questions.stem: Which of the following is a prime number?
questions.explanation: 17 has exactly two factors: 1 and 17. 15 = 3 × 5, 21 = 3 × 7, and 9 = 3 × 3, so they are all composite.

questions.stem: What is the prime factorization of 30?
questions.explanation: 30 = 2 × 15 = 2 × 3 × 5. All three factors (2, 3, 5) are prime, so 2 × 3 × 5 is the prime factorization.

questions.stem: What is the prime factorization of 18?
questions.explanation: 18 = 2 × 9 = 2 × 3 × 3. Both 2 and 3 are prime, so 2 × 3 × 3 is the prime factorization.

questions.stem: Which of these numbers is composite?
questions.explanation: 27 = 3 × 9 = 3 × 3 × 3, so it has more than two factors. 23, 29, and 31 are all prime.

questions.stem: Two students found different factor trees for 60. Student A started with 2 × 30. Student B started with 6 × 10. Which statement is true?
choices: Both get the same prime factorization: 2 × 2 × 3 × 5
Only Student A gets the correct prime factorization
Only Student B gets the correct prime factorization
They will get different prime factorizations
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic says every composite number has exactly one prime factorization. No matter how you start the factor tree, you always end with 2 × 2 × 3 × 5.

questions.stem: What is the prime factorization of 84?
questions.explanation: 84 = 2 × 42 = 2 × 2 × 21 = 2 × 2 × 3 × 7. All factors are prime.

questions.stem: Which number has the prime factorization 2³ × 5?

questions.stem: What is the GCF of 8 and 12?
questions.explanation: Factors of 8: 1, 2, 4, 8. Factors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. The largest common factor is 4.

questions.stem: Which pair of numbers has a GCF of 5?
questions.explanation: 15 = 3 × 5, 25 = 5 × 5. Both share the factor 5, and 5 is the largest shared factor.

questions.stem: What is the GCF of 14 and 21?
questions.explanation: Factors of 14: 1, 2, 7, 14. Factors of 21: 1, 3, 7, 21. The largest common factor is 7.

questions.stem: What is the GCF of 9 and 15?
questions.explanation: Factors of 9: 1, 3, 9. Factors of 15: 1, 3, 5, 15. The largest common factor is 3.

questions.stem: A teacher has 36 pencils and 48 erasers. She wants to make identical supply bags with no items left over. What is the greatest number of bags she can make?
questions.explanation: GCF(36, 48) = 12. She can make 12 bags, each with 3 pencils and 4 erasers.

questions.stem: What is the GCF of 24 and 36?
questions.explanation: 24 = 2³ × 3 and 36 = 2² × 3². The common factors are 2² × 3 = 12.

questions.stem: What is the GCF of 18 and 30?
questions.explanation: 18 = 2 × 3² and 30 = 2 × 3 × 5. Common factors: 2 × 3 = 6.

questions.stem: A baker has 16 muffins and 24 cookies. She makes identical gift boxes using all the treats. What is the greatest number of boxes?
questions.explanation: GCF of 16 and 24 is 8, so she can make 8 equal boxes.

questions.stem: Which pair of numbers has a GCF of 1?
questions.explanation: 8 = 2³ and 15 = 3 × 5 share no common prime factors, so the GCF is 1.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Factor"?
questions.explanation: 3 for the number 12: 12 ÷ 3 = 4 with no remainder.

questions.stem: Which statement about "Greatest Common Factor" is true?
choices: The greatest common factor of 12 and 18 is 2.
The greatest common factor of 12 and 18 is 6.
The greatest common factor of 12 and 18 is 36.
questions.explanation: Correct: The greatest common factor of 12 and 18 is 6.

questions.stem: What is the LCM of 3 and 5?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 3: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, ... Multiples of 5: 5, 10, 15, ... The smallest common multiple is 15.

questions.stem: What is the LCM of 6 and 8?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 6: 6, 12, 18, 24, ... Multiples of 8: 8, 16, 24, ... The smallest common multiple is 24.

questions.stem: What is the LCM of 4 and 10?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, ... Multiples of 10: 10, 20, ... The smallest common multiple is 20.

questions.stem: What is the LCM of 2 and 7?
questions.explanation: Since 2 and 7 share no common factors (they are relatively prime), LCM = 2 × 7 = 14.

questions.stem: Two buses leave the station at 8:00 AM. Bus A returns every 15 minutes and Bus B returns every 20 minutes. When is the next time both buses are at the station together?
questions.explanation: LCM(15, 20) = 60 minutes. Both buses return together 60 minutes after 8:00 AM, at 9:00 AM.

questions.stem: One bus leaves every 12 minutes and another every 15 minutes. They leave together at 9:00. When do they next leave together?
questions.explanation: LCM of 12 and 15 is 60, so they leave together again 60 minutes later.

questions.stem: Which statement about "Least common multiple" is true?
choices: The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 24.
The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 2.
The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 12.
questions.explanation: Correct: The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 12.

questions.stem: A square pyramid has a base edge of 5 in and a slant height of 7 in. What is the surface area?
questions.explanation: Base: 5 × 5 = 25 in². Each lateral face: ½ × 5 × 7 = 17.5 in². Four faces: 4 × 17.5 = 70 in². SA = 25 + 70 = 95 in².

questions.stem: How many lateral (triangular) faces does a square pyramid have?

questions.stem: What is the area of one triangular face with base 8 in and slant height 6 in?

questions.stem: A square pyramid has a base area of 49 cm² and a total lateral area of 84 cm². What is the surface area?

questions.stem: A square pyramid has a base that is 4 by 4 and 4 triangular faces each 10 sq units. What is the surface area?

questions.stem: How many triangular faces does a square pyramid have?

questions.stem: A triangular pyramid (all faces triangles) has 4 faces each with area 9 cm². What is the surface area?

questions.explanation: Total fruit = 6 + 2 = 8. The part-to-whole ratio of bananas to total fruit is 2:8.

questions.stem: In a class of 30 students, 18 are girls. What is the part-to-part ratio of boys to girls?
questions.explanation: Boys = 30 − 18 = 12. Part-to-part ratio of boys to girls = 12:18. (Order matters!)

questions.stem: A pet store has 5 dogs and 9 cats. What is the ratio of dogs to cats?
questions.explanation: The ratio of dogs to cats compares dogs first, then cats: 5 to 9, or 5:9.

questions.stem: A fruit salad has 5 pieces of melon, 3 pieces of pineapple, and 2 grapes. What is the ratio of pineapple to melon?
questions.explanation: The ratio of pineapple to melon compares pineapple (3) to melon (5), so the ratio is 3:5.

questions.stem: A parking lot has 10 cars and 6 trucks. What is the part-to-whole ratio of trucks to total vehicles?
questions.explanation: Total vehicles = 10 + 6 = 16. The ratio of trucks to total is 6:16.

questions.stem: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. What is the ratio of blue to total marbles?
questions.explanation: There are 3 blue and 5 + 3 = 8 total, so blue to total is 3 to 8.

questions.stem: In a class the ratio of boys to girls is 4:5. Which statement must be true?
choices: For every 4 boys there are 5 girls
There are more boys than girls
questions.explanation: A 4:5 ratio means 4 boys for every 5 girls. The actual totals could be 8:10, 12:15, etc.

questions.stem: A recipe uses 2 cups of rice to 3 cups of water. What is the ratio of rice to water?
questions.explanation: Rice to water is 2 to 3, written 2:3.

questions.stem: There are 6 cats and 9 dogs. What is the ratio of cats to dogs in simplest form?
questions.explanation: 6:9 divides by 3 to give 2:3.

questions.explanation: The ratio of pineapple to melon compares pineapple (3) to melon (5), so the ratio is 3:5.

questions.stem: A recipe uses 3 eggs for every 5 cups of flour. How many eggs are needed for 15 cups of flour?
questions.explanation: The scale factor is 15 ÷ 5 = 3. Multiply the eggs by 3: 3 × 3 = 9 eggs.

questions.stem: Which row does NOT belong in a ratio table for 4:5?
questions.explanation: 6:10 simplifies to 3:5, not 4:5. The other ratios (8:10, 12:15, 16:20) all simplify to 4:5.

questions.stem: A ratio table shows 3:7, 6:14, and 9:21. What is the next row?
questions.explanation: The pattern adds 3 to the first value and 7 to the second each time. After 9:21: 9+3=12 and 21+7=28. The next row is 12:28.

questions.stem: A ratio table for 2:5 starts with 2:5, 4:10. What is the third row?
questions.explanation: The additive pattern adds 2 to the first value and 5 to the second. 4+2=6 and 10+5=15. The third row is 6:15.

questions.stem: In a ratio table, the ratio of sugar to butter is 3:2. If you use 12 cups of sugar, how many cups of butter do you need?
questions.explanation: Scale factor: 12 ÷ 3 = 4. Multiply butter by 4: 2 × 4 = 8 cups of butter.

questions.stem: A ratio table shows 5:8, 10:16, 15:24. What scale factor was used from the first row to the third row?
questions.explanation: From 5 to 15: 15 ÷ 5 = 3. From 8 to 24: 24 ÷ 8 = 3. The scale factor is 3.

questions.stem: A ratio table shows 3 tickets cost $12. How much do 7 tickets cost?
questions.explanation: Each ticket costs $12 ÷ 3 = $4, so 7 tickets cost 7 × $4 = $28.

questions.stem: In a ratio table, 4 maps to 10. What does 12 map to?
questions.explanation: 12 is 3 times 4, so multiply 10 by 3 to get 30.

questions.stem: A ratio table keeps the ratio 5:8. If one row is 15, what is its partner value?
questions.explanation: 15 is 3 times 5, so the partner is 3 times 8 = 24.

questions.stem: Which statement about "Equivalent ratio" is true?
questions.explanation: Correct: 2:3 and 4:6 are equivalent ratios.

questions.stem: A ratio table shows (2, 6), (4, 12), and (6, 18). Which ordered pair comes next if the pattern continues?
questions.explanation: The pattern adds 2 to x and 6 to y each time. After (6, 18): x = 6+2 = 8, y = 18+6 = 24. The next point is (8, 24).

questions.stem: When you graph equivalent ratios, the points will always form what shape?
choices: A straight line through the origin
questions.explanation: Equivalent ratios are proportional, so their graph is always a straight line that passes through the origin (0, 0).

questions.stem: A ratio table shows (2, 8), (3, 12), and (5, 20). What is the y-value when x = 7?
questions.explanation: The ratio is y/x = 8/2 = 4. For every x, y = 4x. When x = 7: y = 4 × 7 = 28.

questions.stem: Which point would NOT be on the graph of the ratio 1:5?
questions.explanation: For the ratio 1:5, every y-value should be 5 times the x-value. (3, 10): 5 × 3 = 15, not 10. So (3, 10) does not belong.

questions.stem: Points from a ratio table are plotted at (1, 4), (2, 8), and (3, 12). What is the ratio of y to x for each point?
questions.explanation: For each point: 4÷1 = 4, 8÷2 = 4, 12÷3 = 4. The ratio of y to x is always 4:1.

questions.stem: Two students graph ratio tables. Student A plots (1,2), (2,4), (3,6). Student B plots (1,3), (2,6), (3,9). Whose line is steeper?
questions.explanation: Student A's ratio is 1:2 (y goes up 2 for each 1 in x). Student B's ratio is 1:3 (y goes up 3 for each 1 in x). A higher rate of change means a steeper line, so Student B's is steeper.

questions.stem: A graph of a ratio passes through (0,0) and (2,6). What point also lies on the line?
questions.explanation: The ratio is 6 to 2 = 3 to 1, so (4, 12) follows the same pattern.

questions.stem: On a ratio graph each apple costs $2. Which point shows 5 apples?
questions.explanation: 5 apples at $2 each is $10, so the point is (5, 10).

questions.stem: A line through the origin contains (3, 9). What is the unit rate (y per 1 x)?
questions.explanation: 9 ÷ 3 = 3, so y increases by 3 for each 1 unit of x.

questions.stem: Why do graphs of equivalent ratios start at the origin (0,0)?
choices: Because 0 of one quantity pairs with 0 of the other
Because every line starts at zero
questions.explanation: When you have 0 of one amount you have 0 of the other, so the line passes through (0,0).

questions.stem: Which ratio is equivalent to 2:5?
questions.explanation: Multiply both parts by 2: 2×2 = 4 and 5×2 = 10, so 4:10 is equivalent to 2:5.

questions.stem: A recipe uses 4 eggs for every 6 cups of flour. Which ratio is equivalent?
questions.explanation: Divide both by 2: 4÷2 = 2 and 6÷2 = 3. The ratio 2:3 is equivalent to 4:6.

questions.stem: Which ratio is NOT equivalent to 6:9?
questions.explanation: 6:9 simplifies to 2:3. Check each: 2:3 ✓, 12:18 = 2:3 ✓, 18:27 = 2:3 ✓. But 3:5 does not simplify to 2:3.

questions.stem: What is the simplest form of the ratio 15:25?
questions.explanation: The GCF of 15 and 25 is 5. Divide both by 5: 15÷5 = 3 and 25÷5 = 5. The simplest form is 3:5.

questions.stem: Marcus earns $45 for every 3 hours of work. How much does he earn in 8 hours?
questions.explanation: Unit rate: $45 ÷ 3 = $15/hour. In 8 hours: $15 × 8 = $120.

questions.stem: Which ratio is equivalent to 3:4?
questions.explanation: Multiply both parts of 3:4 by 3 to get 9:12.

questions.stem: Which ratio is NOT equivalent to 2:5?
questions.explanation: 4:9 is not 2:5 doubled (that would be 4:10). The others all reduce to 2:5.

questions.stem: If 12:18 is written in simplest form, what is it?
questions.explanation: 12:18 divides by 6 to give 2:3.

questions.stem: A paint mix uses 3:5 blue to white. To keep the same color with 15 parts white, how much blue?
questions.explanation: 15 white is 3 times 5, so blue is 3 times 3 = 9.

questions.stem: Which statement about "Equivalent ratios" is true?
questions.explanation: Correct: 6:9 and 2:3 are equivalent ratios.

questions.stem: Chef A uses 2 cups of cheese for every 8 crackers. Chef B uses 3 cups of cheese for every 9 crackers. Who uses more cheese per cracker?
questions.explanation: Chef A: 2÷8 = 0.25 cups per cracker. Chef B: 3÷9 ≈ 0.33 cups per cracker. Chef B uses more cheese per cracker.

questions.stem: Which ratio is greater: 3:4 or 5:8?
questions.explanation: Convert to the same denominator: 3:4 = 6:8. Since 6:8 > 5:8, the ratio 3:4 is greater.

questions.stem: Which lemonade recipe is more lemony? Recipe A: 2 lemons for 6 cups of water. Recipe B: 3 lemons for 7 cups of water.
questions.explanation: Recipe A: 2÷6 ≈ 0.33 lemons per cup. Recipe B: 3÷7 ≈ 0.43 lemons per cup. Since 0.43 > 0.33, Recipe B is more lemony.

questions.stem: Which is the better deal: 4 apples for $3 or 6 apples for $5?
questions.explanation: Deal 1: $3÷4 = $0.75 per apple. Deal 2: $5÷6 ≈ $0.83 per apple. $0.75 < $0.83, so 4 apples for $3 is the better deal.

questions.stem: Store A sells 3 mangoes for $6. Store B sells 5 mangoes for $9. Which store offers a better price per mango?
questions.explanation: Store A: $6÷3 = $2.00 per mango. Store B: $9÷5 = $1.80 per mango. Store B has the better (lower) price per mango.

questions.stem: Store A sells 3 pens for $6. Store B sells 5 pens for $9. Which is the better buy?
questions.explanation: Store A is $2 per pen; Store B is $1.80 per pen. Store B is cheaper.

questions.stem: Which ratio is greater: 4:5 or 7:10?
questions.explanation: 4:5 = 8:10, which is greater than 7:10.

questions.stem: Recipe X uses 2 cups sugar per 8 cookies. Recipe Y uses 3 cups per 10 cookies. Which is sweeter per cookie?
questions.explanation: X = 0.25 cup/cookie; Y = 0.30 cup/cookie. Y has more sugar per cookie.

questions.stem: Team A won 6 of 10 games. Team B won 9 of 15 games. Which team has the better win ratio?
questions.explanation: 6:10 = 3:5 and 9:15 = 3:5, so the win ratios are equal.

questions.stem: Which statement about "Equivalent ratio" is true?
questions.explanation: Correct: 4:5 and 8:10 are equivalent ratios.

questions.stem: A recipe uses 4 cups of broth for every 10 servings. How many cups of broth are needed for 30 servings?
questions.explanation: Scale factor: 30 ÷ 10 = 3. Multiply broth by 3: 4 × 3 = 12 cups.

questions.stem: Are the ratios 6:9 and 2:3 equivalent?
questions.explanation: Divide 6:9 by 3 to get 2:3. Since both ratios simplify to 2:3, they are equivalent.

questions.stem: If 3 pounds of apples cost $6, how much do 7 pounds cost?
questions.explanation: Unit rate: $6 ÷ 3 = $2 per pound. For 7 pounds: $2 × 7 = $14.

questions.stem: Solve the proportion: 2/5 = x/20. What is x?
questions.explanation: Scale factor: 20 ÷ 5 = 4. Multiply: 2 × 4 = 8. So x = 8.

questions.stem: A chef uses the proportion 3/7 = x/28 to scale a recipe. What is x?
questions.explanation: Scale factor: 28 ÷ 7 = 4. Multiply: 3 × 4 = 12. So x = 12.

questions.stem: A car travels 150 miles using 5 gallons. At this rate, how far on 8 gallons?
questions.explanation: 150 ÷ 5 = 30 miles per gallon; 30 × 8 = 240 miles.

questions.stem: If 4 workers build a wall in the same time pattern, and 4 workers lay 20 bricks per minute, how many bricks do 10 workers lay per minute at the same rate?
questions.explanation: 20 ÷ 4 = 5 bricks per worker; 5 × 10 = 50 bricks per minute.

questions.stem: A car travels 150 miles in 3 hours. A bus travels 200 miles in 5 hours. Which vehicle is faster?
questions.explanation: Car: 150÷3 = 50 mph. Bus: 200÷5 = 40 mph. The car travels faster at 50 mph vs. 40 mph.

questions.stem: A store sells 3 shirts for $36. How much would 5 shirts cost at the same rate?
questions.explanation: Each shirt is $12, so 5 shirts cost $60.

questions.stem: A recipe for 4 people uses 6 eggs. How many eggs for 10 people?
questions.explanation: 6 ÷ 4 = 1.5 eggs per person; 1.5 × 10 = 15 eggs.

questions.stem: A runner covers 12 km in 4 hours. How long to cover 21 km at the same speed?

What is the first step when finding the area of a trapezoid?
choices: Subtract the shorter base from the longer base
questions.explanation: The formula is A = ½ × (b₁ + b₂) × h. The first step is to add the two parallel bases.

questions.stem: A trapezoid has bases of 5 in and 9 in, and a height of 6 in. What is its area?
A trapezoid has parallel sides 6 cm and 10 cm and height 4 cm. What is its area?
A trapezoid has bases 8 m and 12 m and height 5 m. What is its area?
A trapezoid has area 36 in² and bases 5 in and 7 in. What is its height?
Why is the trapezoid area formula ½(b1 + b2)h?
choices: It averages the two bases, then multiplies by the height
It uses only the longer base
questions.explanation: ½(b1 + b2) is the average of the two parallel sides, multiplied by the height.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Trapezoid"?
choices: A four-sided shape with one pair of parallel sides
questions.explanation: A four-sided shape with one pair of parallel sides: It has exactly one pair of parallel sides.

questions.stem: What is the area of a triangle with base 10 cm and height 6 cm?
questions.explanation: A = ½ × b × h = ½ × 10 × 6 = 30 square centimeters.

questions.stem: A triangle has an area of 24 sq ft and a base of 8 ft. What is the height?
Why do we divide by 2 when finding the area of a triangle?
choices: A triangle is exactly half of a rectangle with the same base and height
The base is always twice the height
We always divide area formulas by 2
questions.explanation: A triangle with base b and height h fits inside a rectangle with the same base and height. The triangle covers exactly half the rectangle, so A = ½ × b × h.

questions.stem: What is the area of a triangle with base 14 ft and height 6 ft?
A triangle has base 10 cm and height 6 cm. What is its area?
questions.explanation: Area = ½ × base × height = ½ × 10 × 6 = 30 cm².

questions.stem: A triangle has area 24 m² and base 8 m. What is its height?
Why does a triangle's area use ½ × base × height?
choices: A triangle is half of a parallelogram with the same base and height
The height is always half the base
questions.explanation: Two identical triangles form a parallelogram, so a triangle is half its area.

choices: Two lines meeting at a 90° angle
questions.explanation: Two lines meeting at a 90° angle: Perpendicular lines form a right angle.

questions.stem: A regular hexagon is divided into 6 equal triangles from the center. Each triangle has a base of 6 ft and a height of 5.2 ft. What is the area of one triangle?
questions.explanation: A = ½ × b × h = ½ × 6 × 5.2 = 15.6 square feet.

questions.stem: Using the triangle from the previous question, what is the total area of the hexagonal skylight?
questions.explanation: Total area = 6 triangles × 15.6 sq ft = 93.6 square feet.

questions.stem: A regular hexagon is split into 6 triangles, each with base 4 cm and height 3.5 cm. What is the total area?
questions.explanation: Each triangle: ½ × 4 × 3.5 = 7 sq cm. Total: 6 × 7 = 42 sq cm.

questions.stem: How many triangles can you make from the center of a regular pentagon?
questions.explanation: A regular pentagon has 5 sides, so drawing lines from the center to each vertex creates 5 equal triangles.

questions.stem: A regular hexagon has 6 equal triangles, each with area 8 cm². What is the hexagon's area?
A regular pentagon is split into 5 equal triangles. If the whole area is 60 in², what is one triangle's area?
questions.explanation: 60 ÷ 5 = 12 in² per triangle.

questions.stem: Why can a regular polygon's area be found by splitting it into congruent triangles?
choices: All the triangles are the same size, so you can multiply one triangle's area by the number of sides
questions.explanation: A regular polygon divides into equal triangles, so total area = (one triangle) × (number of triangles).

questions.stem: A regular octagon splits into 8 triangles, each with area 5.5 m². What is the total area?
Which one is an example of "Regular Polygon"?
choices: A rectangle that is not a square
questions.explanation: A square: All sides and all angles are equal.

questions.stem: An L-shaped room is made of two rectangles: 10 ft × 6 ft and 4 ft × 3 ft. What is the total area?
questions.explanation: Area 1 = 10 × 6 = 60 sq ft. Area 2 = 4 × 3 = 12 sq ft. Total = 60 + 12 = 72 sq ft.

questions.stem: A rectangular patio is 15 ft × 10 ft with a 5 ft × 4 ft rectangular flower bed cut out. What is the remaining area?
questions.explanation: Patio = 15 × 10 = 150 sq ft. Cutout = 5 × 4 = 20 sq ft. Remaining = 150 − 20 = 130 sq ft.

questions.stem: Find the area of an L-shape made of a 5×3 rectangle and a 2×4 rectangle.
When should you subtract areas instead of adding them to find the area of a composite figure?
choices: When a piece is cut out or removed from a larger shape
When one shape is larger than the other
questions.explanation: Subtract when a piece is removed from a larger shape (like a window cut from a wall). Add when two shapes are joined together (like an L-shape).

questions.stem: A figure is a 6×4 rectangle with a 2×2 square added on. What is the total area?
questions.explanation: Rectangle 6 × 4 = 24, plus square 2 × 2 = 4. Total = 28 sq units.

questions.stem: A composite shape is a 10×8 rectangle with a 3×4 rectangle cut out. What is the area?
questions.explanation: 80 − 12 = 68 sq units.

questions.stem: What is the best first step to find the area of a composite figure?
choices: Break it into simple shapes like rectangles and triangles
questions.explanation: Decompose the figure into simple shapes, find each area, then add or subtract.

questions.stem: A garden is a 12×5 rectangle plus a triangle with base 12 and height 4 on top. What is the total area?
questions.explanation: Rectangle 12 × 5 = 60; triangle ½ × 12 × 4 = 24. Total = 84 sq units.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Composite Figure"?
choices: An L-shaped room made of two rectangles
questions.explanation: An L-shaped room made of two rectangles: It is built from two basic shapes.

questions.stem: What is the value of 4³?
What is the value of 6²?
Which expression shows 5³ as repeated multiplication?
questions.explanation: 5³ means use 5 as a factor 3 times: 5 × 5 × 5. The exponent tells how many times to multiply, not what to multiply by.

What is 10⁴?
Which expression equals 81?

questions.explanation: The 4 in 3⁴: It shows 3 is multiplied 4 times.

questions.explanation: The 4 in 3⁴: It shows 3 is multiplied 4 times.

questions.stem: Evaluate 3x + 7 when x = 4.
Evaluate 20 − 2n when n = 6.
Which expression means '5 less than twice a number n'?
questions.explanation: Twice n is 2n. Five less than that is 2n − 5.

questions.stem: Evaluate 4m + 1 when m = 5.
Evaluate 3 + 4 × 2².
questions.explanation: Order of operations: 2² = 4, then 4 × 4 = 16, then 3 + 16 = 19.

questions.stem: Evaluate (8 − 3)² + 6.
If a = 4, what is 2a + 5?
Evaluate 24 ÷ (2 + 4) × 3.
Which one is an example of "Expression"?
questions.explanation: 3x + 5: It has terms and operations but no equal sign.

questions.stem: Which expression represents 'the product of 6 and a number n'?
questions.explanation: 'Product' means multiplication, so the product of 6 and n is 6n.

questions.stem: Which expression represents '9 less than a number y'?
questions.explanation: 'Less than' means subtract from the number, so it's y − 9.

questions.stem: Which expression represents 'the sum of a number m and 15'?
questions.explanation: 'Sum' means addition, so the sum of m and 15 is m + 15.

questions.stem: Which key word tells you to multiply?
questions.explanation: 'Product' means multiplication. 'Sum' means addition, 'difference' means subtraction, and 'quotient' means division.

questions.stem: Which expression means '5 more than a number n'?
questions.explanation: 'More than' means addition: n + 5.

questions.stem: Which expression means 'the product of 7 and a number x'?
questions.explanation: 'Product' means multiplication: 7x.

questions.stem: Write 'a number y divided by 4' as an expression.
questions.explanation: 'Divided by 4' means y/4.

questions.stem: Which expression means '3 less than twice a number m'?
questions.explanation: Twice a number is 2m; '3 less' subtracts 3: 2m − 3.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Coefficient"?
questions.explanation: The 7 in 7n: It is the number multiplied by the variable n.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Constant"?
questions.explanation: The 5 in 3x + 5: It is a number with no variable.

questions.stem: Which property is shown? 5 + 13 = 13 + 5
questions.explanation: The order of the addends changed, so this is the Commutative Property of Addition.

questions.stem: Which property is shown? (6 × 3) × 2 = 6 × (3 × 2)
questions.explanation: The grouping (parentheses) changed but the order stayed the same, so this is the Associative Property of Multiplication.

questions.stem: Which property is shown? 47 + 0 = 47
questions.explanation: Adding 0 does not change the value, so this is the Identity Property of Addition.

questions.stem: Which property lets you rearrange 8 × 5 to 5 × 8?
questions.explanation: Changing the order of factors is the Commutative Property of Multiplication.

questions.stem: Which property is shown by 4 + 7 = 7 + 4?
questions.explanation: Changing the order of addends shows the Commutative Property.

questions.stem: Which property is shown by (2 × 3) × 5 = 2 × (3 × 5)?
questions.explanation: Changing the grouping shows the Associative Property.

questions.stem: What number completes the Identity Property: 9 × ___ = 9?
questions.explanation: Multiplying by 1 keeps the value the same.

questions.stem: Which equation shows the Distributive Property?
questions.explanation: Distributing 3 over (x + 2) gives 3x + 6.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Commutative Property"?
questions.explanation: 4 + 7 = 7 + 4: Changing the order keeps the same sum.

questions.stem: Expand 6(n + 3) using the distributive property.
Expand 4(5 − 2) using the distributive property.
Expand 3(x + 4) using the distributive property.
When using the distributive property with a(b + c), you must multiply 'a' by:
questions.explanation: The distributive property says a(b + c) = ab + ac. You multiply the outside factor by EACH term inside the parentheses.

questions.stem: Use the Distributive Property: 4(x + 3) = ?
Use the Distributive Property: 5(2x − 1) = ?
Which expression is equal to 6x + 18 using a common factor?
questions.explanation: The GCF of 6x and 18 is 6: 6(x + 3).

questions.stem: Use the Distributive Property to compute 7 × 104.

questions.explanation: 3(x + 4) = 3x + 12: The 3 is multiplied by each term inside.

questions.stem: Which expression is equivalent to 4x + 3x?
questions.explanation: 4x + 3x = 7x. Combine the coefficients: 4 + 3 = 7.

questions.stem: Which expression is equivalent to 2(m + 5)?
questions.explanation: 2(m + 5) = 2m + 10 using the distributive property.

questions.stem: Which property is shown: 3(x + 7) = 3x + 21?
questions.explanation: Multiplying 3 by each term inside the parentheses is the distributive property.

questions.stem: Simplify: 5n + 2n
questions.explanation: 5n + 2n = 7n. Add the coefficients: 5 + 2 = 7. The variable stays as n.

questions.stem: Which expression is equivalent to 3x + 3x?
questions.explanation: Adding like terms: 3x + 3x = 6x.

questions.stem: Which expression is equivalent to 2(x + 4)?
questions.explanation: Distribute: 2x + 8.

questions.stem: Are 4x + 2 and 2(2x + 1) equivalent?
choices: Yes, they are equal for every value of x
questions.explanation: 2(2x + 1) = 4x + 2, so they are equivalent for all x.

questions.stem: Which expression is NOT equivalent to 5x + 10?
questions.explanation: 5x + 5 is different; the others all equal 5x + 10.

questions.stem: Which statement about "Equivalent" is true?
questions.explanation: Correct: 2x + 3x and 5x are equivalent expressions.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Like Terms"?
questions.explanation: 4x and 9x: Both have the same variable x to the first power.

questions.stem: Simplify 7x + 3x.
questions.explanation: 7x + 3x = 10x. Add the coefficients: 7 + 3 = 10.

questions.stem: Simplify 9n + 4 + 2n.
questions.explanation: 9n + 2n = 11n. The constant 4 has no like term, so it stays. Result: 11n + 4.

questions.stem: Which pair are like terms?
questions.explanation: 6x and 2x are like terms because they both have the same variable (x) raised to the same power (1).

questions.stem: Simplify 5a + 3 + 2a.
questions.explanation: 5a + 2a = 7a. The constant 3 stays. Result: 7a + 3.

questions.stem: Simplify: 4x + 3 + 2x.
questions.explanation: Combine like terms 4x + 2x = 6x, then add 3: 6x + 3.

questions.stem: Simplify: 7y − 2y + 5.
Simplify: 3(x + 2) + 4x.
Simplify: 8a + 4 − 3a − 1.

questions.explanation: 3y and 8y: They share the same variable y.

questions.stem: Which equation represents: 'A number plus 12 equals 30'?
questions.explanation: 'Plus' means addition, so the equation is n + 12 = 30.

questions.stem: Which equation represents: 'Seven less than a number is 18'?
questions.explanation: 'Seven less than a number' means start with the number and subtract 7: n − 7 = 18.

questions.stem: Which equation represents: 'Three times a number equals 21'?
questions.explanation: 'Three times' means multiply by 3, so the equation is 3n = 21.

questions.stem: Which equation represents: 'A number divided by 4 equals 8'?
questions.explanation: 'Divided by 4' means division: n / 4 = 8.

questions.stem: A detective found some fingerprints on Monday and 14 more on Tuesday, for a total of 30. Which equation represents this?
questions.explanation: The detective's Monday fingerprints (f) plus 14 more equals 30: f + 14 = 30.

questions.stem: Which equation means 'a number x plus 7 equals 12'?
questions.explanation: 'Plus 7 equals 12' is x + 7 = 12.

questions.stem: A book costs $9 more than a pen. The book costs $14. Which equation finds the pen's price p?
questions.explanation: Pen plus 9 equals 14: p + 9 = 14.

questions.stem: Which equation represents '3 times a number equals 21'?
questions.explanation: '3 times a number' is 3n, equal to 21.

questions.stem: Tickets cost $8 each. Which equation finds the number of tickets t bought for $48?
questions.explanation: Cost per ticket times number of tickets equals total: 8t = 48.

questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 9: m = 63 ÷ 9 = 7. Check: 9 × 7 = 63 ✓

questions.stem: A detective splits 54 clues equally among 9 case files. The equation 9c = 54 represents this. What is c?
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 9: c = 54 ÷ 9 = 6 clues per file.

questions.stem: Solve: 6x = 54.
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 6: x = 54 ÷ 6 = 9.

questions.stem: Solve: x/4 = 7.
questions.explanation: Multiply both sides by 4: x = 28.

questions.stem: Solve: 8n = 2.
Five friends split a bill equally and each pays $12. Which equation finds the total t?
questions.explanation: Total divided by 5 equals 12, so t = 60.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Inverse operation"?
choices: Multiplying by 4 to undo multiplying by 4
Dividing by 4 to undo multiplying by 4

questions.stem: Which inequality represents: 'The temperature is less than 40 degrees'?
questions.explanation: 'Less than' means <, so the inequality is t < 40.

questions.stem: Which inequality represents: 'You must be at least 48 inches tall to ride'?
questions.explanation: 'At least' means greater than or equal to, so h ≥ 48.

questions.stem: Which inequality represents: 'A store can hold no more than 75 people'?
questions.explanation: 'No more than' means less than or equal to, so p ≤ 75.

questions.stem: Which inequality represents: 'The speed limit is more than 25 mph'?
questions.explanation: 'More than' means strictly greater than, so s > 25.

questions.stem: A parking garage charges if your stay is more than 2 hours. Which inequality represents times t that are charged?
questions.explanation: 'More than 2 hours' means strictly greater than: t > 2. Exactly 2 hours is not charged.

questions.stem: Which inequality means 'a number x is at least 10'?
questions.explanation: 'At least 10' means 10 or more: x ≥ 10.

questions.stem: Which inequality means 'you must be more than 5 feet tall to ride'?
questions.explanation: 'More than 5' is h > 5.

questions.stem: A bus holds at most 40 people. Which inequality shows the number p of people allowed?
questions.explanation: 'At most 40' means 40 or fewer: p ≤ 40.

questions.stem: Which inequality means 'a number n is less than 8'?
questions.explanation: 'Less than 8' is n < 8.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Inequality"?
questions.explanation: x > 5: It compares values with a greater-than sign.

questions.stem: Which graph represents x < 5?
questions.explanation: x < 5 means 5 is NOT included (open circle) and values less than 5 are shaded (left).

questions.stem: Which graph represents x ≥ 3?
questions.explanation: x ≥ 3 means 3 IS included (closed circle) and values greater than or equal to 3 are shaded (right).

questions.stem: Which graph represents x > 8?
questions.explanation: x > 8 means 8 is NOT included (open circle) and values greater than 8 are shaded to the right.

questions.stem: Which graph represents x ≤ 10?
questions.explanation: x ≤ 10 means 10 IS included (closed circle) and values less than or equal to 10 are shaded (left).

questions.stem: A number line shows a closed circle at 8 and is shaded to the left. Which inequality does this represent?
questions.explanation: Closed circle means the value is included (≤ or ≥). Shaded left means less than or equal to: x ≤ 8.

questions.stem: On a number line, x > 3 is shown with...
choices: An open circle at 3, shaded to the right
A closed circle at 3, shaded left
A closed circle at 3, shaded right
An open circle at 3, shaded left
questions.explanation: 'Greater than' uses an open circle and shades right (larger values).

questions.stem: Which inequality has a closed (filled) circle on its graph?
questions.explanation: 'Less than or equal to' includes the endpoint, shown by a closed circle.

questions.stem: A graph shows a closed circle at -2 shaded to the right. Which inequality is it?
questions.explanation: Closed circle means 'or equal to'; shaded right means greater: x ≥ -2.

questions.stem: Which value is a solution to x < 4?
questions.explanation: Only 3 is less than 4.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Open circle"?
questions.explanation: An open circle on x > 3: 3 is not included when the symbol is >.

questions.stem: Solve: x + 6 > 14
questions.explanation: Subtract 6 from both sides: x > 14 − 6 = 8. Graph: open circle at 8, shade right.

questions.stem: Solve: x − 9 ≤ 3
questions.explanation: Add 9 to both sides: x ≤ 3 + 9 = 12. Graph: closed circle at 12, shade left.

questions.stem: Solve: x + 10 ≥ 25
questions.explanation: Subtract 10 from both sides: x ≥ 25 − 10 = 15. Graph: closed circle at 15, shade right.

questions.stem: Solve: x − 4 < 11
questions.explanation: Add 4 to both sides: x < 11 + 4 = 15. Graph: open circle at 15, shade left.

questions.stem: Is x = 5 a solution to x + 4 > 12?
choices: No, because 5 + 4 = 9, and 9 is not greater than 12
questions.explanation: Substitute: 5 + 4 = 9. Since 9 > 12 is false, x = 5 is NOT a solution.

questions.stem: Solve and choose the solution: x + 3 < 9.
questions.explanation: Subtract 3 from both sides: x < 6.

questions.stem: Solve: 2x ≥ 10.
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 2: x ≥ 5.

questions.stem: A rider must be at least 48 inches tall. Which inequality and a valid height fit?
questions.explanation: 'At least 48' is h ≥ 48, and 50 satisfies it.

questions.stem: Which value is NOT a solution of x ≤ 7?
questions.explanation: 8 is greater than 7, so it is not a solution.

questions.stem: A museum has some paintings. After adding 12, they have 45. Which equation models this?
questions.explanation: 'Adding 12' to the original number gives 45: p + 12 = 45. Solve: p = 33.

questions.stem: A detective needs more than 8 hours to finish the investigation. She has already worked 3 hours. Which inequality represents the additional hours h she needs?
questions.explanation: 3 hours plus additional hours h must be more than 8: 3 + h > 8. Solve: h > 5.

questions.stem: Which model is correct for: 'A number divided by 6 equals 7'?
questions.explanation: Divided by 6 is n / 6. Equals 7 means = 7. So n / 6 = 7.

questions.stem: A bus can carry at most 48 passengers. There are already 31 on board. Which inequality shows how many more can board?
questions.explanation: 'At most 48' means the total must be ≤ 48. So 31 + p ≤ 48. Solve: p ≤ 17.

questions.stem: A student solved 4n = 52 and got n = 13. Is the answer reasonable if n represents the number of notebooks in a box?
choices: Yes — 13 notebooks per box is reasonable
Yes — but only if n is a fraction
questions.explanation: 4 × 13 = 52 ✓. 13 notebooks per box is a reasonable whole number answer.

questions.stem: A taxi charges $3 plus $2 per mile. The ride cost $15. Which equation finds the miles m?
questions.explanation: Base $3 plus $2 per mile: 2m + 3 = 15, so m = 6 miles.

questions.stem: Solve: 4x = 28. How many items if each is $4 and total is $28?
questions.explanation: 28 ÷ 4 = 7 items.

questions.stem: You have $50 and spend $8 per ticket. Which inequality shows how many tickets t you can buy?
questions.explanation: Total spent must be 50 or less: 8t ≤ 50 (so t ≤ 6 whole tickets).

questions.stem: Solve: x − 12 = 20.
questions.explanation: Add 12 to both sides: x = 32.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a statistical question?
choices: How many points did each player score this season?
How many points are scored for a free throw?
What sport does this team play?
How many halves are in a soccer game?
questions.explanation: "How many points did each player score?" is statistical because different players will have different scores — there is variability in the data.

questions.stem: Which question is NOT a statistical question?
choices: How many innings are in a baseball game?
How many home runs did each player hit this year?
How many miles does each runner train per week?
How tall is each player on the basketball team?
questions.explanation: "How many innings are in a baseball game?" has one fixed answer (9) — no variability. The other questions all produce different answers for different people.

questions.stem: Why is 'How many hours of sleep did each student get last night?' a statistical question?
choices: Because different students got different amounts of sleep
Because the answer is always 8 hours
questions.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability — different students slept different amounts, so the data will vary.

questions.stem: A coach asks: "How many hours does each athlete on the track team sleep the night before a meet?" Why is this a statistical question?
choices: Because the answers will vary from athlete to athlete
Because it has a numerical answer
questions.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability. Different athletes sleep different amounts, so the data collected will vary.

questions.stem: Which is a statistical question?
choices: How tall are the students in our class?
questions.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability — heights vary across students.

questions.stem: Which question is NOT statistical?
choices: What time did the bus arrive today?
How many minutes do students sleep each night?
How long do people wait at the store?
What ages are the players on the team?
questions.explanation: A single bus arrival time has one answer, so it is not statistical.

questions.stem: Why do statistical questions expect different answers?
choices: Because the data values vary from one case to another
Because they are always about numbers
Because they only have one answer
questions.explanation: Statistical questions anticipate variability in the data.

questions.stem: Which rewrite makes 'How old am I?' a statistical question?
choices: How old are the students in this school?
How old is my best friend?
questions.explanation: Asking about many students produces data that varies.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Statistical Question"?
choices: How tall are the students in my class?
questions.explanation: How tall are the students in my class?: The answers vary, so data must be collected.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a statistical question?
choices: How many points did each player score this season?
How many points are scored for a free throw?
What sport does this team play?
How many halves are in a soccer game?
questions.explanation: "How many points did each player score?" is statistical because different players will have different scores — there is variability in the data.

questions.stem: Which question is NOT a statistical question?
choices: How many innings are in a baseball game?
How many home runs did each player hit this year?
How many miles does each runner train per week?
How tall is each player on the basketball team?
questions.explanation: "How many innings are in a baseball game?" has one fixed answer (9) — no variability. The other questions all produce different answers for different people.

questions.stem: Why is 'How many hours of sleep did each student get last night?' a statistical question?
choices: Because different students got different amounts of sleep
Because the answer is always 8 hours
questions.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability — different students slept different amounts, so the data will vary.

questions.stem: A coach asks: "How many hours does each athlete on the track team sleep the night before a meet?" Why is this a statistical question?
choices: Because the answers will vary from athlete to athlete
Because it has a numerical answer
questions.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability. Different athletes sleep different amounts, so the data collected will vary.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Statistical Question"?
choices: How tall are the students in my class?
questions.explanation: How tall are the students in my class?: The answers vary, so data must be collected.

questions.stem: Find the mean of: 10, 14, 8, 12, 16
Find the median of: 3, 7, 2, 9, 5
questions.explanation: Ordered: 2, 3, 5, 7, 9. The middle value is 5.

questions.stem: Find the mode of: 4, 7, 4, 9, 2, 4, 8
questions.explanation: 4 appears three times — more than any other value. Mode = 4.

questions.stem: Find the mean of: 6, 8, 10, 12, 14
Data set: 4, 6, 6, 8, 10, 12. What is the median?
questions.explanation: With 6 values, the median is the average of the 3rd and 4th values: (6 + 8) ÷ 2 = 7.

questions.stem: What is the mean of 4, 6, 8, 10, 12?
What is the median of 3, 7, 9, 12, 15?
questions.explanation: The middle value of five ordered numbers is the third one: 9.

questions.stem: What is the mode of 2, 5, 5, 7, 9, 5?
questions.explanation: 5 appears most often (three times).

questions.stem: What is the median of 4, 8, 10, 14?
questions.explanation: With four values, average the middle two: (8 + 10) ÷ 2 = 9.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Outlier"?
questions.explanation: 98 in the set 2, 4, 5, 6, 98: It is far from the other values.

questions.stem: The mean of a data set is 15. One value is 11. What is the absolute deviation of that value from the mean?
questions.explanation: Deviation = 11 − 15 = −4. Absolute deviation = |−4| = 4.

questions.stem: A data set has absolute deviations of 3, 1, 5, 2, 4. What is the MAD?
questions.explanation: Sum of absolute deviations = 3 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 4 = 15. MAD = 15 ÷ 5 = 3.

questions.stem: The mean of a data set is 20. A value is 26. What is the absolute deviation?
questions.explanation: Deviation = 26 − 20 = 6. Absolute deviation = |6| = 6.

questions.stem: Team A has MAD = 2.1 points. Team B has MAD = 6.8 points. Which team is more consistent?
choices: Team A — lower MAD means scores are closer to the mean
Team B — higher MAD means better performance
questions.explanation: Lower MAD means less spread — Team A's scores stay closer to their average, making them more consistent.

questions.stem: Two runners have the same average time of 60 seconds. Runner A's MAD is 1.2 seconds. Runner B's MAD is 5.8 seconds. Which runner is the coach more likely to pick for a relay race that needs a reliable time?
choices: Runner A — lower MAD means more consistent times
Runner B — higher MAD means faster potential
Either — they have the same average
Neither — MAD doesn't matter for relay races
questions.explanation: Runner A's MAD of 1.2 means times are usually within 1.2 seconds of 60. Runner B's times vary more widely. For reliability, pick Runner A.

questions.stem: The data 5, 7, 9 has mean 7. What is the mean absolute deviation (MAD)?
questions.explanation: Distances from 7 are 2, 0, 2; mean = 4 ÷ 3 ≈ 1.33.

questions.stem: What does a larger MAD tell you about a data set?
choices: The values are more spread out from the mean
questions.explanation: MAD measures average distance from the mean; larger MAD means more spread.

questions.stem: The data 10, 10, 10, 10 has what MAD?
questions.explanation: Every value equals the mean, so each distance is 0 and MAD = 0.

questions.stem: For the data 2, 4, 6 (mean 4), what is the MAD?
questions.explanation: Distances are 2, 0, 2; mean = 4 ÷ 3 ≈ 1.33.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Absolute Value"?
questions.explanation: The absolute value of −4 is 4: −4 is 4 units from zero.

questions.stem: A gymnast's scores are: 8.5, 8.8, 8.7, 8.6, 8.9. There are no outliers. Which measure best represents a typical score?
choices: Neither — the data is too small
questions.explanation: The data is symmetric with no outliers, so the mean (8.7) best represents the typical score.

questions.stem: A runner's mile times are: 7:10, 7:15, 7:12, 7:20, 12:00. The 12:00 was due to a cramp. Which measure best represents a typical mile?
choices: Median — the outlier 12:00 pulls the mean too high
Mean — it uses all the data
Mode — it shows the most common time
questions.explanation: The 12:00 is an outlier that pulls the mean up. The median (7:15) better represents the runner's typical time.

questions.stem: Data set: 5, 6, 7, 7, 8, 50. The mean is about 13.8 and the median is 7. Which better represents the typical value?
choices: Median (7) — the outlier 50 inflates the mean
questions.explanation: The mean (13.8) is higher than 5 of the 6 values because the outlier 50 pulls it up. The median (7) better represents a typical value.

questions.stem: A baseball player's batting averages over 6 seasons are: .280, .295, .290, .285, .300, .110. The .110 was an injury-shortened season. Which measure better represents the player's typical batting average?
choices: Median, because the .110 outlier pulls the mean down
Mean, because it uses all the data
Mean, because .110 is a real season
questions.explanation: The .110 is an outlier that pulls the mean down to .260. The median (.2875) better represents typical performance because it isn't affected by the extreme value.

questions.stem: A data set has an extreme outlier. Which measure of center is usually best?
questions.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers, so it better represents typical values.

questions.stem: Which measure of spread pairs naturally with the mean?
questions.explanation: MAD measures spread around the mean.

questions.stem: Test scores are 80, 82, 85, 88, 12. Why is the median better than the mean here?
choices: The score of 12 is an outlier that pulls the mean down
questions.explanation: The outlier 12 lowers the mean a lot, but the median stays near the typical scores.

questions.stem: For data with no outliers and a symmetric shape, which center is appropriate?
questions.explanation: With no outliers, the mean represents the data well.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Skewed"?
choices: Most values low with a few very high values
Values spread evenly around the center
questions.explanation: Most values low with a few very high values: The data has a tail, so it is skewed.

questions.stem: A box plot shows: Min = 10, Q1 = 15, Median = 20, Q3 = 28, Max = 35. What is the interquartile range (IQR)?
On a box plot, what does the line inside the box represent?
questions.explanation: The line inside the box of a box plot always represents the median.

questions.stem: What percentage of data falls between Q1 and Q3 on a box plot?
questions.explanation: The box in a box plot contains the middle 50% of the data (from Q1 to Q3).

questions.stem: Find the range of: 14, 22, 8, 31, 17
questions.explanation: Range = max − min = 31 − 8 = 23.

questions.stem: Two box plots show: Team A has IQR = 8, Team B has IQR = 22. What does this tell you?
choices: Team A's middle 50% is more consistent; Team B's is more spread out
Team B is a better team
Team A has a higher median
The IQRs don't tell you about consistency
questions.explanation: A smaller IQR means the middle 50% of data is more tightly clustered. Team A's scoring is more consistent in the middle range.

questions.stem: In a box plot, the line inside the box represents the...
questions.explanation: The middle line of a box plot marks the median.

questions.stem: A box plot has Q1 = 20 and Q3 = 35. What is the interquartile range (IQR)?
What does the box (between Q1 and Q3) of a box plot represent?
questions.explanation: The box spans Q1 to Q3, holding the middle half of the data.

questions.stem: A data set has minimum 5 and maximum 45. What is the range?
questions.explanation: Range = maximum − minimum = 45 − 5 = 40.

questions.stem: A histogram shows these frequencies: 0–4: 3 players, 5–9: 8 players, 10–14: 12 players, 15–19: 5 players. How many players are represented in total?
questions.explanation: Add all frequencies: 3 + 8 + 12 + 5 = 28 players.

questions.stem: In a histogram, what does the height of each bar represent?
choices: The frequency (count) of data in that interval
The average of the data in that interval
questions.explanation: Each bar's height shows the frequency — how many data values fall within that interval.

questions.stem: How is a histogram different from a regular bar graph?
choices: Histogram bars touch (no gaps) because the intervals are continuous ranges
Histograms use colors and bar graphs don't
Bar graphs show numbers and histograms show words
questions.explanation: In a histogram, bars touch because each interval connects to the next (0–9, 10–19, etc.) — the data is continuous.

questions.stem: A histogram has intervals: 0–4, 5–9, 10–14, 15–19. What is the width of each interval?
questions.explanation: Each interval covers 5 values (0,1,2,3,4 then 5,6,7,8,9 etc.), so the width is 5.

questions.stem: A histogram of test scores shows: 50–59: 2, 60–69: 5, 70–79: 10, 80–89: 8, 90–99: 3. Which interval has the most students?
questions.explanation: The 70–79 interval has the highest frequency of 10 students.

questions.stem: A histogram differs from a bar graph because...
choices: It groups numeric data into equal intervals with no gaps
questions.explanation: Histograms display continuous numeric data in intervals (bins) with bars touching.

questions.stem: A histogram bin for 10–19 has a bar height of 6. What does 6 represent?
choices: 6 data values fall in that interval
questions.explanation: The bar height is the frequency — how many values fall in that bin.

questions.stem: Histogram bins are 0–9, 10–19, 20–29 with heights 4, 9, 2. How many data values total?
questions.explanation: 4 + 9 + 2 = 15 data values.

questions.stem: Why should histogram intervals be equal in width?
choices: So the bars fairly compare how many values fall in each range
questions.explanation: Equal widths make frequency comparisons fair across bins.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Histogram"?
choices: A graph comparing favorite colors with gaps between bars
A graph with bars for the ranges 0–9, 10–19, 20–29
questions.explanation: A graph with bars for the ranges 0–9, 10–19, 20–29: It groups data into equal intervals.

questions.stem: A histogram of basketball players' ages shows: most players are 22–28, with a long tail of older players up to age 40. What is the shape of this distribution?
questions.explanation: Most data is on the left (younger ages) with a tail stretching right (older ages). This is skewed right.

questions.stem: A data set is symmetric. What is likely true about the mean and median?
choices: The mean is much higher than the median
The median is much higher than the mean
They are always exactly the same
questions.explanation: In a symmetric distribution, the mean and median are approximately equal because the data is balanced on both sides.

questions.stem: Which word describes the direction of the TAIL, not the peak?
choices: Skew — the direction of the long tail tells you the skew direction
Skew — the direction of the peak tells you the skew direction
Skew — the direction the median points
Skew — the direction of the mode
questions.explanation: The skew direction is named after the TAIL. If the tail stretches to the right, it is skewed right, even though the peak is on the left.

questions.stem: A dot plot of goals scored per game shows: 0(5 dots), 1(7 dots), 2(4 dots), 3(2 dots), 4(1 dot). What is the shape?
choices: Skewed right — most values at the low end with a tail to the right
Skewed left — most values at the high end
Uniform — all bars the same height
questions.explanation: The data peaks at 0–1 goals and tails off toward 4. Most data is on the left with fewer values stretching right — this is skewed right.

questions.stem: A distribution with a long tail stretching to the right is called...
questions.explanation: A tail pulling toward larger values means it is skewed right.

questions.stem: A data set that is roughly the same on both sides of the center is...
questions.explanation: If both sides mirror each other, the distribution is symmetric.

questions.stem: In a right-skewed distribution, how does the mean compare to the median?
choices: The mean is usually greater than the median
The median is always larger by 10
questions.explanation: The long right tail pulls the mean above the median.

questions.stem: A distribution with two clear peaks is described as...
questions.explanation: Two peaks means the distribution is bimodal.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Symmetric"?
choices: Data with a long tail to the right
Data balanced evenly around the center
questions.explanation: Data balanced evenly around the center: Both sides match, so it is symmetric.

questions.stem: Which ordered pair names a point 3 units right and 7 units up from the origin?
questions.explanation: The first coordinate is horizontal (right 3), the second is vertical (up 7). So (3, 7).

questions.stem: What are the coordinates of the origin?
questions.explanation: The origin is where the x-axis and y-axis cross, at (0, 0).

questions.stem: A point is at (6, 2). What does the 6 tell you?
questions.explanation: The first number (x-coordinate) tells you horizontal movement. 6 means go right 6 units from the origin.

questions.stem: In which quadrant is the point (-3, 5)?
questions.explanation: Negative x and positive y place a point in Quadrant II.

questions.stem: What are the coordinates of a point 4 units right and 2 units down from the origin?
questions.explanation: Right is positive x, down is negative y: (4, -2).

questions.stem: A point lies on the x-axis at x = -6. What is its y-coordinate?
questions.explanation: Points on the x-axis always have y = 0, so the point is (-6, 0).

questions.stem: In which quadrant is the point (7, -1)?
questions.explanation: Positive x and negative y place a point in Quadrant IV.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Integer"?
questions.explanation: −3: It is a whole number's opposite with no fraction.

questions.stem: Which ordered pair names a point 3 units right and 7 units up from the origin?
questions.explanation: The first coordinate is horizontal (right 3), the second is vertical (up 7). So (3, 7).

questions.stem: What are the coordinates of the origin?
questions.explanation: The origin is where the x-axis and y-axis cross, at (0, 0).

questions.stem: A point is at (6, 2). What does the 6 tell you?
questions.explanation: The first number (x-coordinate) tells you horizontal movement. 6 means go right 6 units from the origin.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Integer"?
questions.explanation: −3: It is a whole number's opposite with no fraction.

questions.stem: What is the absolute value of -9?
questions.explanation: Absolute value is the distance from zero. -9 is 9 units from zero, so |-9| = 9.

questions.stem: Which integer has the GREATEST absolute value: -3, 7, -5, 2?
questions.explanation: |-3| = 3, |7| = 7, |-5| = 5, |2| = 2. The greatest absolute value is 7.

questions.stem: What is |0|?
questions.explanation: Zero is 0 units from itself on the number line, so |0| = 0.

questions.stem: Which statement about absolute value is TRUE?
choices: |-4| = |4| because both are 4 units from zero
questions.explanation: Absolute value measures distance from zero. Both -4 and 4 are 4 units from zero, so |-4| = |4| = 4.

questions.stem: Which integer represents '12 degrees below zero'?
questions.explanation: 'Below zero' is negative: -12.

questions.stem: What is |6| + |-4|?
A diver is at -30 feet and a bird at +30 feet. What is true about their distances from sea level?
choices: They are the same distance from 0
questions.explanation: |-30| = |30| = 30, so both are 30 feet from sea level.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Integer"?
questions.explanation: −7: It is the opposite of a whole number.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Absolute value"?
questions.explanation: The absolute value of −5 is 5: −5 is 5 units from zero.

questions.stem: Which statement is TRUE?
questions.explanation: -5 is farther to the left on the number line than -2, so -5 < -2 is true.

questions.stem: Which integer is the LEAST: -3, 5, -8, 2, 0?
questions.explanation: -8 is farthest to the left on the number line, making it the least (smallest) integer.

questions.stem: What is |−8|?
questions.explanation: Absolute value is distance from 0, always positive. |−8| = 8.

questions.stem: Three cities have temperatures of -12°F, -5°F, and 3°F. Which city is warmest?
questions.explanation: 3°F is the greatest because it is farthest to the right on the number line. The order from coldest to warmest is -12, -5, 3.

questions.stem: Order from least to greatest: -3, 1, -7, 4.
questions.explanation: Most negative first: -7, -3, 1, 4.

questions.stem: Which temperature is the coldest?
questions.explanation: -8° is the lowest value, so it is coldest.

questions.stem: Which symbol makes this true: -10 ___ -4?
questions.explanation: -10 is less than -4, so the symbol is <.

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Negative integer"?
questions.explanation: −6: It is an integer less than zero.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Which point is located between -1 and -2 on the number line?
questions.explanation: -1.5 is between -1 and -2 because it is 0.5 units to the left of -1 (or 0.5 units to the right of -2).

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Which fraction is equivalent to 0.25?
questions.explanation: 0.25 means 25 hundredths = 25/100 = 1/4.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Where is -3/4 located on the number line?
questions.explanation: -3/4 = -0.75, which is between 0 and -1. It is 3/4 of the way from 0 toward -1.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Which rational number is closest to 0 on the number line?
questions.explanation: |-1/4| = 0.25, |0.5| = 0.5, |-2| = 2, |1.75| = 1.75. Since 0.25 is the smallest absolute value, -1/4 is closest to zero.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Where is -1.5 located on a number line?
questions.explanation: -1.5 sits halfway between -1 and -2.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Which rational number is greatest?
questions.explanation: 3/4 = 0.75 is the largest of the four values.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: What is the opposite of -2/3?
questions.explanation: The opposite of -2/3 is 2/3, the same distance on the other side of 0.

questions.lessonTitle: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
questions.stem: Order from least to greatest: -0.5, 0.25, -1, 0.75.
questions.explanation: Most negative first: -1, -0.5, 0.25, 0.75.

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: In which quadrant is the point (-4, 5)?
questions.explanation: The x-coordinate is negative (left) and the y-coordinate is positive (up). Left and up is Quadrant II.

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: A point has a positive x-coordinate and a negative y-coordinate. Which quadrant is it in?
questions.explanation: Positive x (right) and negative y (down) = Quadrant IV (bottom-right).

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: Which point is located on the x-axis?
questions.explanation: A point is on the x-axis when its y-coordinate is 0. (4, 0) is on the x-axis.

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: Point A is at (-5, 3). If you move 8 units to the right, what are the new coordinates?
questions.explanation: Moving right means adding to the x-coordinate: -5 + 8 = 3. The y stays the same. New point: (3, 3).

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: In which quadrant is the point (-4, -6)?
questions.explanation: Negative x and negative y place a point in Quadrant III.

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: Point A is (5, 3). Which point shares the same x-coordinate?
questions.explanation: (5, -2) has the same x-coordinate, 5.

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: Which ordered pair is in Quadrant IV?
questions.explanation: Quadrant IV has positive x and negative y: (8, -3).

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: The point (0, -7) lies on which axis?
questions.explanation: With x = 0, the point is on the y-axis.

questions.lessonTitle: Ordered Pairs in All Four Quadrants
questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Negative coordinate"?
questions.explanation: The −3 in (−3, 5): It is a coordinate less than zero.

questions.stem: What is the distance between (-4, 3) and (2, 3)?
questions.explanation: Both points have y = 3, so this is a horizontal distance. |2 - (-4)| = |2 + 4| = 6 units.

questions.stem: What is the distance between (5, -2) and (5, 4)?
questions.explanation: Both points have x = 5, so this is a vertical distance. |4 - (-2)| = |4 + 2| = 6 units.

questions.stem: A rectangle has vertices at (1, 1), (5, 1), (5, 4), (1, 4). What is its perimeter?
questions.explanation: Width = 5 - 1 = 4. Height = 4 - 1 = 3. Perimeter = 2(4 + 3) = 14.

questions.stem: Two points are at (-3, -7) and (-3, 5). What is the distance between them?
questions.explanation: Same x = -3. Distance = |5 - (-7)| = |5 + 7| = 12 units.

questions.stem: What is the distance between (2, 3) and (2, 9)?
questions.explanation: Same x, so subtract y-values: 9 − 3 = 6 units.

questions.stem: What is the distance between (-4, 1) and (5, 1)?
questions.explanation: Same y; distance = |5 − (-4)| = 9 units.

questions.stem: A point is at (-3, 2) and another at (-3, -5). How far apart are they?
questions.explanation: Same x; distance = |2 − (-5)| = 7 units.

questions.stem: Why can you find the distance between (1, 4) and (6, 4) by subtracting x-values?
choices: They have the same y-coordinate, so the segment is horizontal
Distance is always the larger number
You must always add the coordinates
questions.explanation: Equal y-values mean a horizontal segment, so distance = |6 − 1| = 5.

questions.stem: What is the reflection of (4, -3) over the x-axis?
questions.explanation: Reflecting over the x-axis changes the sign of the y-coordinate. The x stays the same (4), and the y changes from -3 to 3. The answer is (4, 3).

questions.stem: What is the reflection of (-2, 6) over the y-axis?
questions.explanation: Reflecting over the y-axis changes the sign of the x-coordinate. x goes from -2 to 2, and y stays 6. The answer is (2, 6).

questions.stem: Which point is on the y-axis?
questions.explanation: Points on the y-axis have x-coordinate = 0. (0, 5) is on the y-axis.

questions.stem: Point A is at (-3, 5). It is reflected over the x-axis to A', then A' is reflected over the y-axis to A''. What are the coordinates of A''?
questions.explanation: Over x-axis: (-3, 5) → (-3, -5). Then over y-axis: (-3, -5) → (3, -5). Both coordinates changed sign.

questions.stem: Reflect (3, 5) across the x-axis. What is the image?
questions.explanation: Reflecting across the x-axis changes the sign of the y-coordinate: (3, -5).

questions.stem: Reflect (-2, 4) across the y-axis. What is the image?
questions.explanation: Reflecting across the y-axis changes the sign of the x-coordinate: (2, 4).

questions.stem: A point (6, -1) is reflected across the x-axis. Which coordinate changes sign?
questions.explanation: Reflecting across the x-axis flips the sign of y only, giving (6, 1).

questions.stem: Reflect (-4, -7) across the y-axis. What is the image?
questions.explanation: Reflecting across the y-axis changes the sign of x: (4, -7).

questions.stem: Which one is an example of "Reflection"?
questions.explanation: (3, 4) flipped over the x-axis to (3, −4): It is a mirror image across the axis.
